figures.caption: Table 4: Average PQ for each nuclear category across the three PanNuke splits. N, E, I, C, and D represent Neoplastic, Epithelial, Inflammatory, Connective, and Dead, respectively.
Table 3: Comparison of Precision (P), Recall (R), and F1-score (F1) for nuclei detection and classification across all categories. Results are averaged over the three PanNuke splits.

Figure 1: This figure illustrates our motivation. Compared to the segmentation results of a full fine-tuned SAM (second row), our CFR-SAM achieves superior precision, particularly in capturing faint boundaries and distinguishing between densely clustered objects.
Figure 4: The design of the HMFM is presented, highlighting its mechanism for aggregating multi-level encoder features.

Table 1: Performance comparison on the PanNuke dataset for nuclei instance segmentation. Following (Chen et al., 2023b; Hörst et al., 2024), both binary PQ (bPQ) and multi-class PQ (mPQ) are computed for evaluation. Red/Blue represents the best/second performance. Params* denotes trainable parameters of model.

Figure 8: Compared to the segmentation results of a full fine-tuned SAM (second row), our CFR-SAM achieves superior precision, particularly in capturing faint boundaries and distinguishing between densely clustered objects.
Figure 9: Visual comparison of the BGMR ablation study for nuclei instance segmentation. Nuclear types are represented by distinct boundary colors.
Figure 10: Visual comparison of different adapting strategies for nuclei instance segmentation.

Table 7: Ablation study of different modules. All trainable parameters are from the second-stage fine-tuning of SAM. The symbol ∗ indicates that we increase the low-rank dimension to match the trainable parameters.
Table 5: Performance comparison on the CPM-17 dataset for nuclei instance segmentation.
Table 6: Performance comparison on the MoNuSeg dataset for nuclei instance segmentation. † indicates our reproduced results.

Figure 6: Visual comparison of our method and CellViT on the PanNuke dataset for nuclei instance segmentation. Each predicted and GT instance mask is assigned a distinct color at random for visual separation. The red boxes highlight the better results achieved by our model.
Figure 7: Visual comparison of our method and CellViT on the PanNuke dataset for nuclei classification. Nuclear types are represented by distinct boundary colors.
Figure 5: The workflow of the BGMR is depicted, demonstrating the process of integrating boundary cues to refine the initial mask prediction.

Figure 2: The pipeline illustrates the two-stage procedure of CFR-SAM. First, a Prompt Learner predicts nuclei centroids to generate point prompts. Second, a PEFT strategy adapts the SAM for nuclear instance segmentation. The final instance segmentation results are displayed with contours overlaid on each detected nucleus for clarity.
